items.prompt: A singly linked list must be scanned once, using O(1) extra space, to return a middle node. Which state best matches the requirement?
constraints: The list is singly linked and acyclic for this task.
Only O(1) auxiliary references are permitted.

options.text: Advance slow by one next link and fast by two next links per valid iteration.
Binary-search the list by repeatedly jumping to midpoint indices.
Store every node in an array and return the node at index floor(n/2).
Move a pointer only when consecutive payload values differ.

feedback.reason: A 1:2 speed ratio lets slow reach a middle boundary when fast reaches the end, using only two references.
feedback.details: Both pointers follow the same next relation, but fast covers twice as many edges. When fast exhausts the list, slow has covered approximately half the path. An array also finds a middle but uses O(n) extra space; binary search assumes random access; value changes are unrelated to structural distance. The exact even-length convention depends on initialization and loop guard.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.array_then_index: This violates the O(1)-extra-space requirement by storing all node references.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.binary_search_nodes: A singly linked list cannot jump directly to midpoint indices without additional indexing.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.compare_values: Payload equality does not measure how far a pointer has traversed.

items.prompt: slow and fast start at the same head. Each completed loop advances slow once and fast twice. Ignoring termination for the moment, which relation holds after t completed loops?
constraints: Each stated iteration completes both advances.
Distance is measured in traversed next edges.

options.text: Their distances depend on the payload values encountered.
slow has traversed 2t edges and fast has traversed t edges.
slow has traversed t edges and fast has traversed 2t edges.
Both have traversed t edges because they started together.

feedback.reason: The loop's defining invariant is the fixed 1:2 edge-advance ratio.
feedback.details: Starting together sets both initial distances to zero. Every completed iteration adds one edge to slow's distance and two to fast's, so the distances are t and 2t. This relation explains middle finding and relative phase in cycles. Different start nodes or step sizes add an offset, and invalid end dereferences must still be prevented by the guard.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.same_distance: Equal starts do not cancel the different per-iteration step counts.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.2t_and_t: This reverses the declared speeds of the two pointers.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.depends_on_values: Traversal distance is determined by links and step counts, not payload values.

items.prompt: For A -> B -> C -> D, the contract requires the first middle B. slow starts at A and advances one step whenever fast can advance two. Which initialization gives the required phase under the guard fast != null and fast.next != null?
constraints: The list length may be even.
The required even-length result is the first middle.

options.text: Initialize fast at whichever central payload is smaller.
Initialize fast = tail, so slow never moves.
Initialize fast = head, so slow stops at C.
Initialize fast = head.next, so slow stops at B.

feedback.reason: Starting fast one node ahead shifts the phase so slow selects the first middle for an even-length list.
feedback.details: With fast at B, the first loop moves slow to B and fast to D; the next guard fails because D.next is null, so B is returned. With both at A, two valid loops place slow at C, the second middle. Both conventions can be correct under different contracts. The required result must be stated before choosing initialization.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.fast_at_a: This phase returns the second middle C under the stated guard.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.fast_at_d: Starting at the tail does not maintain the intended half-progress relation from the list entry.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.choose_by_values: Middle position is structural and independent of payload ordering.

items.prompt: A middle-finding routine that previously started slow = head and fast = head is changed to start both at a new dummy whose next is head, while keeping the same guard and returning slow. What must be checked?
constraints: The loop guard and return expression are otherwise unchanged.
dummy is not part of the desired logical output.

options.text: The extra starting node changes the phase and may return dummy or a different middle unless the contract is adjusted.
A dummy can never affect any pointer result because it is not a data node.
Every data-node payload is implicitly shifted by one.
The dummy enables direct access to the middle index.

feedback.reason: Relative-speed algorithms depend on start offsets; traversing an extra sentinel changes those offsets even when the sentinel is not logical output.
feedback.details: A sentinel does not alter the data-node chain, but it adds one edge to the traversal path from the chosen start. The slow/fast phase and termination position therefore need to be rederived. The routine might intentionally use a dummy to obtain a predecessor or first-middle convention, but that is a deliberate contract choice, not an automatic equivalence.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.answer_never_changes: Logical membership and algorithmic phase are different; an implementation-only node can still affect traversal offsets.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.values_shift: Adding a predecessor does not mutate payloads.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.random_access_enabled: A sentinel provides one fixed predecessor, not indexed access to arbitrary positions.

items.prompt: slow advances one link and fast advances two until fast reaches the end of an n-node acyclic list. No index or stored node array exists. What are the time and extra-space costs?
constraints: The input is a standard singly linked list.
The algorithm does not modify the list.

feedback.reason: Fast traverses the list's length in a constant fraction of n iterations, and only two pointer references are maintained.
feedback.details: The loop performs roughly n/2 iterations, but asymptotically that is O(n); each iteration does constant work. The method does not access position n/2 directly. slow and fast, plus fixed local state, use O(1) extra space. Building an array of node references would permit indexed lookup afterward but would cost O(n) preprocessing time and O(n) space.

items.prompt: slow references node X(value=5) and fast references a distinct node Y(value=5). Under a fast/slow traversal, has a pointer meeting occurred?
constraints: X and Y are separately allocated nodes.
Duplicate values are allowed in the list.

options.text: It cannot be decided without numeric node indices.
No. Equal payloads do not count; X and Y must be the same node object.
Yes. All nodes in one list have the same node type.
Yes. Equal values are sufficient for convergence.

feedback.reason: Pointer convergence is an identity event, and X and Y are explicitly distinct objects.
feedback.details: Fast and slow may visit many nodes with equal payloads, especially in lists with duplicates. Treating equal values as a meeting can terminate early at unrelated positions. Reference equality directly answers whether both variables designate one structural node; numeric indices are neither intrinsic nor required.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.yes_equal_values: Payload equality can occur at different positions and does not establish a pointer meeting.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.yes_same_type: Sharing a node class or representation says nothing about object identity.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.unknown_without_indices: Reference equality decides the meeting without random-access indices.

items.prompt: One loop iteration should advance slow once and fast twice in an ordinary acyclic list. Order the checks and updates so no invalid next dereference occurs.
constraints: The language evaluates fast.next.next by dereferencing fast and then fast.next.
The list may have even or odd length.

feedback.reason: Both objects needed by the two-link expression must be validated before the fast update is evaluated.
feedback.details: The critical adjacent relation is check_fast -> check_fast_next because fast.next cannot be read when fast is null. check_fast_next must precede advance_fast because the expression reads fast.next.next. Advancing slow after the guards keeps the iteration atomic with respect to the stated condition. Implementations may combine the checks in a short-circuit condition, but the dependency order remains the same.

items.prompt: Select all scenarios where the standard fast/slow relative-speed invariant is directly applicable without redefining pointer movement.
constraints: Direct applicability means the standard fixed-step proof can be used unchanged.
Both correct cases follow node.next deterministically.

options.text: Finding a middle while both pointers repeatedly follow the same node.next relation.
Moving left or right in a sorted array according to whether a sum is too small or too large.
Detecting convergence while both pointers repeatedly follow the same node.next relation inside a cycle.
Moving window boundaries according to a changing validity predicate.

feedback.reason: Middle and cycle traversals use one deterministic successor relation with a fixed speed ratio; the other scenarios use decision-dependent movement rules.
feedback.details: The fast/slow proof tracks relative distance under repeated application of node.next. Sorted pair search and variable windows also use two positions, but their movement is selected from data or validity conditions rather than a fixed 1:2 successor application. Those techniques need different invariants. The presence of two pointers alone is not the transfer signal.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorted_pair_sum: Its pointer moves are chosen by an ordering comparison, not by a fixed relative speed along one successor function.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.variable_window: Window endpoints advance according to validity maintenance, not a constant speed ratio.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.middle_next_chain: This is a canonical case where end progress encodes half progress.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.cycle_next_chain: This is a canonical case where relative phase changes by one per iteration inside the cycle.
